title: Why Propagate Alone? Parallel Use of Labels and Features on Graphs

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.
reviews.main_review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 1: The contributions are neither significant nor novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
This paper examines the "label trick" which enables the parallel propagation of labels and features and benefits various SOTA GNN architectures. The label trick has not yet been rigorously analyzed and so this paper studies it from a theoretical perspective. It first introduces a self-excluded simplification of the label trick by applying an extreme splitting strategy. It then shows that the random splits of the label trick can be regarded as regularization of self-excluded label weights. The paper also discusses broader applications of the label trick in order a) to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and b) to get rid of the randomness effect by incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers.

Main Review:
(1) The effectiveness of the label trick in improving GNN performance has already been demonstrated in prior work. Because of that, this paper does not repeat these efforts and instead tries to showcase the broader application scenarios from Sec 4. I understand this goal. However, results in the four application scenarios that the authors selected indicate little or no benefit of the label trick. In particular, closely examining, Sec 5.1-5.4 and Tables 1-4, I see that using the label trick gives performance comparable to the baseline methods. This happens in many (if not most) scenarios when I take into account the standard deviation across ten independent runs. 

(2) Following from the previous point, I would like to get more clarity on the role of label trick in GNN training. The study makes a very shallow remark that: "For example, at the time of this submission, the top 10 results spanning multiple research teams all rely on the label trick, as do the top 3 results from the recent KDDCUP 2021 Large-Scale Challenge MAG240M-LSC." That is okay, however, it does not mean that the label trick necessarily leads to better performance. To be able to argue that, one would need to run an ablation study comparing top models from the Challenge with and without the label trick. Are there other desirable effects that made the label trick so popular (e.g., faster convergence, more robust training etc.)?

Summary Of The Review:
I appreciate the formal description of the label trick and the associated theoretical analysis. However, the novel use-cases motivated by that analysis are weak and empirical analysis on the role of the label trick is incomplete.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review Report

**Overall Assessment:** Marginally above the acceptance threshold

This paper presents a formal description and theoretical analysis of the "label trick" commonly employed in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). The authors provide valuable insights, including a self-excluded simplification of the trick and an interpretation of its random splits as a regularization mechanism for self-excluded label weights. The theoretical contributions, which aim to empower graph-based methods with trainable weights and eliminate randomness in GNNs with linear propagation layers, are well-appreciated and represent the core strength of this work.

However, despite these theoretical merits, the empirical validation presented in the manuscript raises significant concerns. The paper's argument for the label trick's widespread popularity and perceived benefits (e.g., in top OGB submissions) is not sufficiently substantiated by the empirical analysis.

**Strengths:**

*   **Formal Description and Theoretical Analysis:** The paper provides a rigorous formal description of the label trick, which is a valuable contribution to understanding its mechanics. This is complemented by a thorough theoretical analysis, including the introduction of a self-excluded simplification and the interpretation of random splits as a regularization technique.
*   **Technical Detail:** The analysis delves into the technical specifics of incorporating self-excluded propagation within GNNs composed of linear propagation layers, offering a deeper understanding of the trick's underlying principles.

**Weaknesses:**

*   **Weak Empirical Evidence for Novel Applications:** The results presented across four application scenarios (Section 5.1-5.4, Tables 1-4) indicate little to no significant benefit of the label trick over established baseline methods. Crucially, when considering the standard deviation across multiple runs, the performance gains often appear negligible or inconsistent, making the novel use-cases motivated by the analysis less convincing.
*   **Incomplete Explanation of Widespread Adoption:** The paper does not adequately explain the widespread adoption and perceived effectiveness of the label trick in practice. The argument for its popularity (e.g., in top OGB submissions) is not sufficiently supported by comprehensive ablation studies comparing models with and without the trick to substantiate claims of performance benefits beyond raw accuracy.
*   **Limited Empirical Analysis on the Role of the Label Trick:** The empirical analysis on the precise role and impact of the label trick is incomplete. It fails to fully investigate other desirable effects that may have led to its popularity, such as faster convergence, improved training robustness, or better generalization on specific data distributions.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

*   **Clarify the Precise Role:** The authors should provide more clarity on the precise role and mechanism through which the label trick contributes to GNN training, especially in scenarios where it has shown practical utility.
*   **Conduct Comprehensive Ablation Studies:** To substantiate claims regarding the label trick's performance benefits and widespread adoption, it is highly recommended to conduct an ablation study. This should involve comparing top-performing models from well-known challenges (e.g., OGB, KDDCUP) both with and without the label trick. Such an analysis would offer more compelling evidence for its practical value.
*   **Investigate Other Desirable Effects:** Beyond raw accuracy, the paper should investigate other potential desirable effects that might contribute to the label trick's popularity, such as faster convergence, enhanced training stability, or improved robustness to hyperparameter choices. This would provide a more holistic understanding of its practical utility.

**Conclusion:**

The paper's theoretical examination of the label trick, particularly its formal description, associated analysis, and interpretation of random splits as regularization, is a commendable contribution. However, the empirical evidence presented for its novel applications is largely unconvincing, often showing performance comparable to existing baselines without clear, significant advantages. Furthermore, the paper does not adequately explain the widespread adoption and perceived effectiveness of the label trick in practice, lacking comprehensive empirical studies on its impact beyond raw accuracy. While the theoretical insights are valuable, the empirical validation needs substantial strengthening to fully justify the practical significance of the label trick. Given the strong theoretical foundation but significant empirical shortcomings, the paper is marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors provide a thorough theoretical justification to the recently emerging and heuristically proposed label trick, i.e., adopting a random portion of node labels in the training set as the input node attributes. Specifically, the authors start from the simplest case of label trick and derive the general formulation of the label trick as a regularizer. Motivated by the theoretical analysis, the authors proposed novel use cases of the label trick in broader scenarios.

Main Review:
The work is solid with rigorous formulation, theoretical justification, and novel use cases of the label trick. The authors derive general formulations for the label trick as regularizations in both regression and classification cases. The derivation is presented in a smooth way and provides several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions. The proposed use cases based on the theoretical are interesting and promising according to the empirical evaluations. Overall, I recommend acceptance due to the thorough analysis and potentially broader use cases inspired by the work. However, my assessment might be inaccurate due to my limited expertise in this topic.

Summary Of The Review:
A solid and inspiring theoretical work. Novel use cases are introduced based on theoretical grounding and are justified by promising empirical results.

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
This paper presents a highly commendable and rigorous investigation into the 'label trick,' offering a thorough theoretical justification for this recently emerging and often heuristically applied technique. The authors successfully derive general formulations, interpreting the label trick as a powerful regularizer applicable to both regression and classification tasks. Furthermore, motivated by their deep theoretical analysis, the work proposes novel use cases that are empirically shown to be both interesting and promising. This is a solid, rigorous, and inspiring piece of work that I strongly recommend for acceptance.

**Strengths:**
The strengths of this work are numerous and significant. First and foremost, the paper provides a truly thorough theoretical justification for the 'label trick,' grounding it in a rigorous formulation that clarifies its underlying principles. The derivation of general formulations, positioning the label trick as a regularization technique for both regression and classification scenarios, is presented with remarkable clarity and smoothness, making the complex theoretical concepts accessible. This technical detail is a core strength, transforming a previously heuristically proposed method into a well-justified and broadly applicable technique.

Beyond the foundational theory, the paper offers several inspiring and insightful remarks and conclusions, which effectively broaden the understanding and potential applications of this concept. Motivated by their robust theoretical analysis, the authors propose novel use cases for the label trick in broader scenarios. These proposed use cases are not merely theoretical constructs; they are thoroughly evaluated through empirical studies, demonstrating their effectiveness and promising potential. The analysis itself is so comprehensive that it offers insights inspiring potentially even wider applications of the label trick.

**Weaknesses:**
I found no significant weaknesses in the manuscript that would detract from its overall quality or impact. The work is exceptionally well-executed and presented.

**Suggestions:**
I have no major suggestions for improvement. The paper is well-structured, clearly written, and thoroughly executed, leaving little room for substantial additional recommendations.

**Overall Assessment:**
In summary, this is an excellent paper that makes a substantial contribution to the field. Its combination of rigorous theoretical analysis, clear derivations of general formulations for the label trick as a regularizer, and practical, empirically validated novel use cases makes it a standout piece of work. The paper is solid, rigorous, and highly inspiring, offering both theoretical depth and practical utility. I wholeheartedly recommend its acceptance.

**Decision:** 8: accept, good paper

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.
reviews.main_review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

**Review Summary:**
This paper presents a theoretical analysis of the 'label trick' in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), a technique often employed heuristically. The authors demonstrate that, under certain simplifying assumptions (e.g., linear function f, no node features), the stochastic label trick can be reduced to an interpretable deterministic objective, which comprises a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization factor. This theoretical perspective is then used to motivate and introduce novel applications, including trainable label propagation, deterministic application to GNNs with linear propagation layers (such as SGC, SIGN, and TWIRLS), and trainable Correct and Smooth (C&S). While the work addresses a fundamental problem and proposes a well-motivated approach, the current manuscript faces significant challenges in terms of clarity, theoretical rigor, and comprehensive comparisons, which places it marginally below the acceptance threshold.

**Strengths:**
The paper tackles a fundamental and important problem concerning the effective utilization of labels and features in graph-based prediction. The research connecting classical label propagation methods with contemporary message passing neural networks is a beneficial contribution to the community. The authors' approach is notably well-motivated, particularly their suggestion to retain only the off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix to address the issue of identity mapping in the label trick, which makes Equation (10) intuitively sensible. Overall, providing a theoretical exposition for the 'label trick', which has primarily been used as a heuristic, represents a worthwhile pursuit.

**Weaknesses:**
Despite its commendable goals, the paper exhibits several weaknesses that impede its clarity and impact:

*   **Attribution and Related Work:** Insufficient emphasis or explicit credit is given to the paper (arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf) believed to have first proposed the label trick. Additionally, the paper lacks comparisons to other relevant works that explore the interplay of label and feature propagation, such as Jia & Benson 2021 (arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf). The discussion would also benefit from referencing other papers that discuss learning or modifying the adjacency/propagation matrix.
*   **Clarity and Readability:** The manuscript is at times hard to read due to being notationally dense, particularly with an excessive use of subscripts. The continuous stream of 'Remarks' also detracts from overall readability and ease of understanding.
*   **Theoretical Intuition and Derivation:** Several theoretical explanations require greater clarity. Specifically, the intuition behind Equation (3)—why the column space of Y_tr is expressed by post-multiplying with a weight matrix W—is unclear. Similarly, the intuition behind the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth needs further elaboration. The derivation of Equation (5) is also vague, particularly the transition from the second-last term to the last term, especially considering the expectation over a discrete uniform sample. The subscript notation 'E_splits' used in equations is also difficult to parse.
*   **Experimental and Methodological Details:** Section 4.1 lacks sufficient detail regarding the training procedure and the final model. In Figure 1, the right plot does not include the deterministic label trick for comparison, and the specific GNN used in Section 5.3 is unspecified. The explanation in Remark 4, stating 'no chance for overfitting' when regularization is shut off, is questioned and requires significant clarification. Furthermore, it is unclear whether the theoretically derived regularization term from the deterministic case is applied to the results presented in Table 3.
*   **Technical Inconsistencies:** There is an inconsistency in the use of parentheses versus square brackets in Equations (4) and (5). A minor typo exists in Remark 2, where 'insure' should be corrected to 'ensure'.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**
To address the identified weaknesses and enhance the paper's overall quality, I recommend the following:

*   **Attribution and Related Work:** Provide further emphasis and explicit credit to the paper (arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf) that first proposed the label trick. Include comprehensive comparisons to other relevant works that compare label and feature propagation, such as Jia & Benson 2021, and add references to papers discussing learning or modifying the adjacency/propagation matrix.
*   **Clarity and Readability:** Rewrite the paper for improved clarity, specifically addressing notational density and restructuring the presentation of the 'Remarks' section for better flow.
*   **Theoretical Intuition and Derivation:** Elaborate on the intuition behind Equation (3) and the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth. Provide a more detailed derivation of Equation (5), clarifying the transition from the second-last to the last term, including the expectation over a discrete uniform sample and the 'E_splits' notation.
*   **Experimental and Methodological Details:** Provide greater detail for Section 4.1, thoroughly explaining how the procedure is trained and defining the final model. Clarify why the deterministic label trick is not used for comparison in the right plot of Figure 1 and specify the GNN model used in Section 5.3. Explain the reasoning behind the claim of 'no chance for overfitting' in Remark 4 if regularization is shut off. Clarify if the theoretically derived regularization term is used for the results in Table 3, and if not, provide a clear justification.
*   **Technical Corrections:** For node regression tasks in Table 2, evaluate performance using the R^2 value. Correct the typo 'insure' to 'ensure' in Remark 2. Ensure consistency in the use of parentheses versus square brackets in Equations (4) and (5).

**Overall Assessment and Recommendation:**
This paper addresses an important and fundamental problem, offering a novel theoretical perspective on the 'label trick' in GNNs. The effort to formalize a widely used heuristic is commendable and holds significant potential for the community. However, the current manuscript requires substantial revisions to improve clarity, theoretical rigor, and the thoroughness of its experimental exposition and comparisons. Many core theoretical explanations are difficult to follow, and the experimental setup lacks sufficient detail. Therefore, while the core idea is promising, the paper is **marginally below the acceptance threshold** in its current form. Addressing the identified weaknesses and implementing the suggested improvements would substantially strengthen the work and make it suitable for publication.
reviews.original_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The authors discuss the utilization of labels in graph neural networks (or conversely, the utilization of node features in collective classification algorithms, e.g., label propagation) for node prediction. It expounds on the stochastic 'label trick' whereby training labels are concatenated to node features and are then randomly masked to avoid trivial solutions. With simplifying assumptions (where there are no node features), they show that this procedure is reduced to minimizing fitting and regularization terms, which is used as motivation for a few practical methods.

Main Review:
### Strengths (+) & Weaknesses (–)

(+) The problem that the authors tackle is fundamental/important. The community would benefit from research that connects the ideas of classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks.

(+)  The approach is well-motivated in that one main issue of the label trick is the undesirable solution of the identity mapping, but only keeping off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix is a seemingly simple fix (whereas diagonal terms are fine for feature propagation). That is to say, Equation (10) makes intuitive sense.

(+) The work expounds upon the use of the label trick, and is right in saying that it has been mainly used as a heuristic, seeing effective use. Studying it further is a worthwhile pursuit.

(–) Some further emphasis/credit should be given to https://arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf who (I believe) first proposed the label trick.

(–) What is the intuition behind equation (3), namely why would one want to express the column space of $Y_{tr}$ by post-multiplying with a weight matrix $W$? I wonder similarly for the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.

(–) Given that the paper has some 'theoretical flavor' to it, we should see some comparison to other works that compare label and feature propagation (even if these propagations are not used in parallel), e.g., Jia & Benson 2021 (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).

(–) Some equations are unclear, and the subscript of $\mathbb{E}_{splits}$ is vague/difficult to parse. I would appreciate some elaboration on Equation 5 in particular, how does one go from the second-last term to the last term? Since D_out has cardinality 1, should the expectation not be over a discrete uniform sample from $i \sim \{ 1 \dots n \}$, which would simplify to

$ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in D_{tr}} \ell (y_i, [P(Y_{tr} - Y_i W]_i ) $.

Could you please tell me what I'm missing/failing to see here? Thanks.

More generally, the paper is at times hard to read due to it being notationally dense (particularly the excessive use of subscripts), and the stream of the 'Remarks'. I feel the paper could benefit from one more pass of re-writing.

(–) For Table 2, R^2 value should be used to evaluate regression.

(–) Section 4.1 is unclear and should have greater detail. For example, how is this procedure trained? What is the final model (is it just supervision on the Neumann series with a weight matrix instead of a propagation matrix)? Further, some reference should be given to other papers who learn/modify the adjacency/propagation matrix.

### Questions:

- Why isn't the deterministic label trick used for comparison in the right plot in Figure 1? Further, which GNN is used? I believe this is unspecified in Section 5.3.
- In remark 4, if the regularization is shut off why is there "no chance for overfitting"? 
- If the derivation shows that we can eventually decompose with a regularization term in the deterministic case, is this used for results in Table 3? If it is not used (as it does not seem to be mentioned), why not?

Some less important nitpicks:
- typo in remark 2 "insure" → "ensure"
- inconsistency in use of parentheses vs square brackets in (4) and (5)

Summary Of The Review:
The paper has strengths and tackles an important problem, but needs further work on the points mentioned above.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The paper provides a theoretical analysis of the 'label trick' in Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), demonstrating that its stochastic nature can be reduced to a deterministic objective comprising data-fitting and graph-dependent regularization terms under simplifying assumptions. While the work tackles an important problem and offers a well-motivated approach, the reviewer identifies significant areas for improvement, including clarity in theoretical explanations, more comprehensive comparisons with related work, and overall readability.
strengths: The problem addressed, concerning the utilization of labels and features in graph-based prediction, is fundamental and important.
The research connecting classical label propagation methods and message passing neural networks is beneficial for the community.

The approach is well-motivated, addressing the issue of identity mapping in the label trick by suggesting to keep only off-diagonal terms in the propagation matrix, which makes Equation (10) intuitively sensible.
The work is a worthwhile pursuit as it expounds upon the use of the label trick, which has primarily been used as a heuristic.
weaknesses: Insufficient emphasis or credit given to the paper (arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf) that is believed to have first proposed the label trick.
Lack of clear intuition behind Equation (3), specifically why the column space of Y_tr is expressed by post-multiplying with a weight matrix W.
Lack of clear intuition behind the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.
The paper lacks comparison to other relevant works that compare label and feature propagation, such as Jia & Benson 2021 (arxiv.org/pdf/2101.07730.pdf).
The paper is at times hard to read due to being notationally dense, particularly with excessive use of subscripts and the stream of 'Remarks'.
Section 4.1 is unclear and lacks sufficient detail regarding the training procedure and the final model.
The deterministic label trick is not used for comparison in the right plot of Figure 1, and the specific GNN used in Section 5.3 is unspecified.
The explanation in Remark 4 regarding 'no chance for overfitting' when regularization is shut off is questioned and requires clarification.
It is unclear whether the theoretically derived regularization term from the deterministic case is applied to the results in Table 3.
suggestions: Give further emphasis and credit to the paper (arxiv.org/pdf/2009.03509.pdf) that first proposed the label trick.
Provide elaboration on the intuition behind Equation (3) and the trainable Smoothing step in Correct & Smooth.
Include comparisons to other works that compare label and feature propagation, such as Jia & Benson 2021.
Elaborate on the derivation of Equation (5), specifically how to transition from the second-last term to the last term, considering the expectation over a discrete uniform sample.
Rewrite the paper for improved clarity, addressing notational density and the presentation of the 'Remarks' section.
Use the R^2 value to evaluate regression tasks in Table 2.
Provide greater detail for Section 4.1, explaining how the procedure is trained and defining the final model.
Add references to other papers that discuss learning or modifying the adjacency/propagation matrix.
Clarify why the deterministic label trick is not used for comparison in the right plot of Figure 1 and specify the GNN model used in Section 5.3.
Explain the reasoning behind 'no chance for overfitting' in Remark 4 if regularization is shut off.
Clarify if the theoretically derived regularization term is used for the results in Table 3, and if not, provide a justification.
Correct the typo 'insure' to 'ensure' in Remark 2.
Ensure consistency in the use of parentheses versus square brackets in Equations (4) and (5).
main_points: The paper aims to provide a theoretical foundation for the widely-adopted 'label trick' in GNNs, which has previously been used primarily as a heuristic.

It proves that, under certain simplifying assumptions (e.g., linear function f, no node features), the stochastic label trick can be reduced to an interpretable deterministic training objective composed of a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization factor.

This theoretical perspective is used to motivate and introduce novel use cases for the label trick, including trainable label propagation, deterministic application to GNNs with linear propagation layers (e.g., SGC, SIGN, TWIRLS), and trainable Correct and Smooth (C&S).

The 'label trick' itself involves using a randomly-selected portion of training labels as GNN inputs (concatenated with features) to make predictions on the remaining labels, thereby accommodating parallel use of features and labels.
technical_details: The intuition behind the formulation of Equation (3) is unclear to the reviewer.
The derivation step in Equation (5) from the second-last term to the last term, especially concerning the expectation over a discrete uniform sample, requires elaboration.
The subscript notation 'E_splits' in equations is vague and difficult to parse.
There is an inconsistency in the use of parentheses versus square brackets in Equations (4) and (5).
A typo exists in Remark 2, where 'insure' should be 'ensure'.
The specific GNN architecture used for the right plot in Figure 1 is not specified in Section 5.3.
The paper suggests that for Table 2, which involves node regression tasks, the R^2 value should be used as an evaluation metric.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper:
The paper focus on the label trick.
The label tricks is a method for graph-based semi-supervised learning.
In this setting, we have some labeled data points and some unlabeled data points, the goal is to label the unlabeled data.
The data points can form vertices of a graph, with edges built based on similarity of the points (eventually with a weighting scheme).
Labels can be diffused along the graph to label unlabeled data thanks to graph Laplacian.
A other way to leverage a graph structure is to build a graph neural networks and learn parameters of this networks to match labels of labeled data.
The authors states that the first method is utilizing labels and the second method is utilizing features based on a graphical model. They say that both should be done simultaneously, and argue that this is what the label trick is doing.

Eventually known labels can used along with features as the input of a GNN, but if we only ask to match labels of labeled data, than a trivial mapping can be learnt (the identity from known labels to the labels of labeled data). Therefore it is smart to use a drop-out strategy where we hide few labels (in particular the ground-truth label) when we want to learn the labels of a labeled data. This is the label trick.

The paper tries to understand what the label trick is about by looking at simple models.
It also discussed on its novel-case of applications.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- It is nice to take a step back to tries to understand what might look like wizard engineering, and show that the labels tricks can be understood with some simple models.
- This paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written

Weaknesses:
- This results in this paper are not really satisfactory. The background and motivations are not really well explained. And the duality between stochasticity of the label trick and the deterministic regularization is not really back-up by "to-the-point" experiments. 
- The paper is lacking rigor as I will detail later.
- Some claims of the paper are weird to me, in particular, the way the graph is built should indeed use features knowledge. So graph-based Laplacian do leverage features (yet in an unsupervised way). Also if the node features are noisy or unreliable, which will downgrade the performance of GNN, I believe the graph structure than will also be noisy or unreliable for those nodes if the graph is build based on features similarity.
- For a paper that seeks a theoretical explanation of the usefulness of the label trick. I do not have the impression to have learn a lot from it. For example, why self-excluded prediction do not work as well as full execution of the label trick?
Also, why the label trick is better than GNN with a Laplacian regularization akin Pfau et al. or Cabannes et al. with a MixUp regularization akin Lopez-Paz et al.?


Notes

page 1:
1/ The readibility of the paper would be improved if the authors were to go straight to the point and more rigorous. For example of “straight to the point", at line 4 in the introduction, the fact that labels and features vary smoothly over the graph and i.i.d. are two differents problems. And talking about i.i.d. samples is only adding confusion in the mind of the reader (at least in my mind).
For example of “rigorous", in the abstract, talking about the identity mapping from input to output, I believe the authors talks about the transductive setting, in which the feature X is forgotten but a propagation rule is used, which can be null as explained for label leakage in Eq. (3). Clearly the "identity mapping" from inputs in R^d to outputs in R^c is not well defined.

page 2:
1/ It would be nice to explain more clearly the graph formalism. For example:
Each node is linked to a input point x in R^d, and a label in R^c, from which we can form the node feature matrix (or design matrix) X and the label matrix of the nodes (or label matrix) Y. The graph is a undirected and edge have weights. The adjacency matrix is the matrix of weights (symmetric because the graph is undirected), thus S is symmetric. 

2/ "Nodes connected by an edge […] are likely to share the same labels". If the graph is weighted this depend on the weight. If the graph is unweighted, it should be specified.
 
3/ Eq.(1) I believe the second F should be F_tr, otherwise, there is, using pythagoras a penalization pushing F towards zeros on testing data. A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

reviews.text:
**Review for Paper ID: [Insert Paper ID if available, otherwise omit]**

This paper undertakes the ambitious task of providing a theoretical explanation for the "label trick" in graph-based semi-supervised learning, a technique that combines elements of label propagation and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). The authors aim to demystify this approach, which utilizes stochastic label splitting (dropout) to prevent models from learning trivial identity mappings from input labels, by reducing its stochastic form to a deterministic objective involving a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization factor. While the research direction is promising and the paper is relatively easy to follow, I find the current analysis to be unsatisfactory, primarily due to a lack of rigor, insufficient experimental validation of its theoretical claims, and a failure to clearly demonstrate its superiority over existing methods. Consequently, the paper is marginally below the acceptance threshold in its current form.

**Strengths:**

*   The paper makes a commendable attempt to provide a theoretical understanding of the 'label trick' using simple models. This effort is valuable in moving beyond the perception of this technique as mere 'wizard engineering' and offering a more formal foundation.
*   The manuscript is generally well-written and easy to follow, making the core ideas accessible to readers.

**Weaknesses:**

Despite its strengths, the paper presents several significant shortcomings that prevent it from being ready for publication:

*   **Insufficient Rigor and Explanations:** The theoretical results presented are not entirely satisfactory. The background and motivations for the 'label trick' are not adequately explained, leaving the reader to infer its necessity. More broadly, the paper is lacking in rigor in several instances, which undermines the strength of its theoretical arguments.
*   **Lack of Experimental Validation:** The duality between the stochasticity of the label trick and its proposed deterministic regularization is not sufficiently backed up by 'to-the-point' experiments. Without clear empirical evidence, the theoretical claims regarding this duality remain speculative.
*   **Questionable Claims:** Several claims made in the paper are questionable. For example, the assertion that graph-based Laplacian methods only leverage features in an unsupervised way requires further substantiation. Similarly, the implication that noisy node features would not also lead to a noisy graph structure, especially if the graph is constructed based on feature similarity, seems counter-intuitive and needs clarification.
*   **Limited Insight:** Despite the paper's goal of providing a theoretical explanation, I did not feel I learned much fundamentally new from the presented analysis. The insights offered did not significantly deepen my understanding of the 'label trick' or its underlying mechanisms.
*   **Unexplained Performance Differences:** The paper does not explain why self-excluded prediction, a component of the label trick, does not perform as well as the full execution of the label trick. This is a critical point that needs to be addressed for a comprehensive understanding.
*   **Lack of Comparative Analysis:** A major weakness is the paper's failure to demonstrate the superiority of the 'label trick' compared to existing GNNs that incorporate similar regularization mechanisms, such as Laplacian regularization (e.g., Pfau et al., Cabannes et al.) or MixUp regularization (e.g., Lopez-Paz et al.). Without such a comparison, the practical utility and unique advantages of the label trick remain unclear.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

To bring this paper to an acceptable standard, I recommend the following revisions:

*   **Improve Readability and Rigor:** The paper would benefit from being more direct and rigorous in its explanations. Confusing tangents, such as the discussion of 'i.i.d. samples,' should be avoided or clarified.
*   **Clarify Graph Formalism:** Provide a clearer and more comprehensive explanation of the graph formalism. This should include how nodes link to input features (x in R^d) and labels (R^c), the precise nature of the adjacency matrix (e.g., weights, symmetry), and the implications of graph weighting.
*   **Correct Equation (1):** In Eq.(1), the second 'F' should be changed to 'F_tr' to prevent an unintended penalization effect on testing data. Additionally, specify the dimensions of 'F' (e.g., F ∈ R^{n × c}) and clarify whether the objective can be minimized independently for each coordinate.
*   **Address Typographical Errors:**
    *   Correct the typo 'P ≈ PY' to 'P Y' in the second paragraph of page 3.
    *   Clarify that the approximation for 'P Y' can be an equality with an infinite sum when λS is contracting.
    *   Rephrase the observation 'P smooths the training labels' to appear after the formal definition of 'P' for better logical flow.
    *   Remove unnecessary parentheses (e.g., 'model from (...)') on page 4.
*   **Integrate SGC Discussion:** Include a note on SGC (Simple Graph Convolution) and its relevance, potentially by condensing sections 3.2 and 3.3 to make space.
*   **Clarify 'P' in Eq.(3):** The definition of 'P' in Eq.(3) and how it is fixed needs to be explicitly clarified.
*   **Technical Formatting:** Check the LaTeX log file for 'overboxes' and fix them in the appendix. Standardize the formatting of references, specifically changing 'ArXiv' entries to their respective journal and conference publications where available.

**Conclusion:**

The paper investigates an interesting and relevant topic, aiming to provide a theoretical justification for the 'label trick' by reducing its stochastic form to a deterministic objective with a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization factor. While the attempt to move beyond heuristic understanding is laudable, the current manuscript suffers from a lack of rigor in its theoretical analysis, insufficient experimental validation for its core claims, and a failure to convincingly demonstrate the superiority of the label trick compared to established GNN regularization methods. The insights gained from the current theoretical treatment are also limited. Therefore, while the research direction is valuable, the paper is not yet ready for publication in its current form and requires substantial revisions to address the identified weaknesses.

**Rating:** 5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

A close form solution can still be written with this correct formulation as well as the power method. The fact that Y = 0 is an informative labels (e.g. because Y are logits) and that pushing F towards 0 does not bias the system should be specified.
Also it would be nice to specify F \in R^{n \times c}, and to specify also that this minimizing this objective can be done independently according to each coordinates of \Y = \R^c, if there is no constraints on F.

page 3:
1/ There is a mistake with P \approx PY on the second paragraph. 
Also the approximation can be written as an equality with an inifinite sum since \lambda S is contracting (equality with a series being here understood as the convergence in any norm).

2/ "where […] P smooths the training labels” is weird since P is not used to defined F^(k). This observation should be written after the definition of P = (1 - \lambda)(I - \lambda S)^{-1}.

page 4:
1/ "model from (…)”: the parenthesis should be removed

2/ A note on SGC would be highly appreciated, as well as an explanation on the relevance of such a model. Space can be found by condensing section 3.2. and 3.3.

3/ What is P in Eq.(3), P was defined as “a propagation matrix”, how is it fixed here?

Appendix:
There is several overboxes, please check the log file when compiling latex.

References formatting:
ArXiv -> journal and conference publications

Summary Of The Review:
Overall while having justifications of what people do in practice is nice, I think this paper fall short of providing a satisfactory analysis of the label tricks, especially since it does not rigorously explained it superiority compared to other methods.
I think this stream of research is valuable but this is too early for publication.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

extracted_ideas.summary: The paper aims to theoretically explain the 'label trick' in graph-based semi-supervised learning, which combines label propagation and GNNs. However, the reviewer finds the analysis unsatisfactory, citing a lack of rigor, insufficient experimental validation for its theoretical claims, and a failure to clearly demonstrate its superiority over existing methods.
strengths: Attempts to provide a theoretical understanding of the 'label trick' using simple models, moving beyond its perception as 'wizard engineering'.
The paper is relatively easy to follow and well-written.
weaknesses: The results presented are not entirely satisfactory.
Background and motivations for the label trick are not well explained.
The duality between the stochasticity of the label trick and its deterministic regularization is not sufficiently backed up by 'to-the-point' experiments.
The paper is lacking in rigor in several instances.

Some claims are questioned, particularly that graph-based Laplacian methods only leverage features in an unsupervised way, and the implication that noisy node features would not also lead to a noisy graph structure if the graph is built on feature similarity.
Despite seeking a theoretical explanation, the reviewer did not feel they learned much from the paper.
The paper does not explain why self-excluded prediction does not perform as well as the full execution of the label trick.
The paper fails to demonstrate the superiority of the label trick compared to GNNs with Laplacian regularization (e.g., Pfau et al., Cabannes et al.) or MixUp regularization (e.g., Lopez-Paz et al.).
suggestions: Improve readability by being more direct and rigorous, avoiding confusing tangents like the discussion of 'i.i.d. samples'.

Provide a clearer explanation of the graph formalism, including how nodes link to input features (x in R^d) and labels (R^c), the nature of the adjacency matrix (weights, symmetry), and the implications of graph weighting.
Correct Eq.(1) by changing the second 'F' to 'F_tr' to avoid an unintended penalization effect on testing data.
Specify the dimensions of 'F' (F \in R^{n \times c}) and clarify if the objective can be minimized independently for each coordinate.
Correct the typo 'P \approx PY' to 'P Y' in the second paragraph of page 3.
Clarify that the approximation for 'P Y' can be an equality with an infinite sum when \lambda S is contracting.
Rephrase the observation 'P smooths the training labels' to appear after the formal definition of P.
Remove unnecessary parentheses (e.g., 'model from (...)') on page 4.
Include a note on SGC and its relevance, potentially by condensing sections 3.2 and 3.3 to make space.
Clarify the definition of 'P' in Eq.(3) and how it is fixed.
Check the LaTeX log file for 'overboxes' and fix them in the appendix.
Standardize the formatting of references, specifically changing 'ArXiv' to journal and conference publications.
main_points: The paper investigates the 'label trick,' a method combining label propagation and GNNs to leverage both labels and features in semi-supervised learning.
The 'label trick' employs a stochastic label splitting (dropout) technique to prevent models from learning trivial identity mappings from input labels.

The paper attempts to provide a theoretical justification for the label trick, reducing its stochastic form to a deterministic objective with a data-fitting term and a graph-dependent regularization factor.

The reviewer critically notes that the theoretical analysis lacks sufficient rigor and experimental validation, particularly regarding the regularization effect and the comparison of the label trick's performance against alternative methods.
The reviewer suggests that the paper, while valuable in its research direction, is not yet ready for publication due to these shortcomings.
technical_details: The concept of 'identity mapping' from R^d to R^c in the abstract is ill-defined.
Eq.(1) is believed to have a typo where the second 'F' should be 'F_tr'.
The approximation 'P \approx PY' on page 3 is a typo and should be 'P Y'.
The statement 'P smooths the training labels' on page 3 is oddly placed before 'P' is fully defined.
A minor formatting error involves unnecessary parentheses around 'model from (...)' on page 4.
The identity and fixed nature of 'P' in Eq.(3) are unclear.
The LaTeX compilation produced 'several overboxes' in the appendix.
Reference formatting for 'ArXiv' entries is inconsistent and should be updated to journal/conference publications.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.
reviews.main_review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary Of The Paper:
The paper proposes a deterministic version for the label trick in GNNs (and other graph frameworks), that is based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process in order to avoid label leakage. The authors propose some applications in other label propagation settings. Experiments are shown to compare with regular label propagation, with and without label trick, effect of regularization term in the deterministic setting vs randomized one.

Main Review:
Strong points:
1.	The paper provides a relative new framework for GNNs where the stochastic label trick is replaced by a deterministic alternative which focuses on exclusion of self propagation, the alternative seems to be novel
2.	Showing an upper bound in  Thm 2 on the deterministic proposal with respect to the cross entropy loss is a valuable result.

Weak points:
1.	Motivation: I still feel unconvinced on what are the reasons for replacing the non-deterministic label trick with a deterministic one, except of course under sampling. Is there a computation bottleneck as well? 
2.	The authors claim that prior use of the label trick has not been theoretically substantiated but what is the Deterministic advantage here in terms accuracy/ speed/ any other advantage?
3.	The experimental validation leaves me a bit puzzled: except for the graph in Fig 1 there no actual comparison between the deterministic and SotA random label tricks. The authors instead focus on comparisons that are not directly related to what they propose in the paper (e.g. trainable vs non-trainable, with label trick vs without). Why is that so?
4.	My main concern is that the experimental validation even for the experiments on the trainable version and use of the label trick is not convincing enough. I see that in some examples the difference between either the trainable label propagation vs the traditional one, as well as the use of the label trick do not show significant improvement (if at all) in particular when the STD is taken into account.
5.	There are some missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graph and the incorporation of label data:
a.	Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, Ronald R. Coifman; 9(55):1711−1739, 2008.
6.	Many of the algorithms mentioned are not fully understood and self-contained to be easily addressed by the reader, also what is the reference to C&S?

Summary Of The Review:
Some fundamental issues in the paper are preventing me from accepting this paper: the experimental validation is insufficient and unconvincing. The motivation for proposing the deterministic alternative to the label trick is not discussed.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Peer Review

This paper introduces a novel deterministic variant of the label trick for Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), which employs a self-exclusion operator to prevent label leakage during propagation. This deterministic approach is presented as an alternative to existing stochastic label tricks and explores its applications within label propagation settings. While the paper offers some interesting theoretical insights, I find that the overall experimental validation is unconvincing and the core motivation for replacing stochastic methods with a deterministic one remains insufficiently discussed. Consequently, I recommend that the paper is marginally below the acceptance threshold.

### Strengths

1.  **Novel Framework:** The paper proposes a relatively new framework for GNNs by replacing the stochastic label trick with a deterministic alternative. This alternative, based on a self-exclusion operator for the propagation process, appears to be a novel contribution to the field.
2.  **Theoretical Contribution:** A valuable theoretical result is presented in Theorem 2, which establishes an upper bound on the proposed deterministic method concerning the cross-entropy loss. This provides a formal guarantee for the deterministic proposal.

### Weaknesses

1.  **Insufficient Motivation:** A primary weakness is the lack of a sufficiently convincing motivation for opting for a deterministic label trick over its non-deterministic counterparts. The paper does not adequately articulate the practical advantages, such as significant computational speed-ups, accuracy improvements, or other demonstrable benefits, beyond merely avoiding sampling. The supposed deterministic advantage in terms of accuracy, speed, or other metrics compared to prior stochastic label tricks is not clearly substantiated.
2.  **Unconvincing Experimental Validation:** The experimental validation is largely insufficient and unconvincing. There is a notable absence of direct comparisons between the proposed deterministic label trick and state-of-the-art random (stochastic) label tricks, with the exception of a single instance in Figure 1.
3.  **Irrelevant Experimental Focus:** Many experiments focus on comparisons not directly related to the core proposal of the deterministic label trick itself (e.g., comparing trainable vs. non-trainable versions, or with label trick vs. without). This detracts from demonstrating the unique value and efficacy of the deterministic approach.
4.  **Lack of Significant Improvement:** The experimental validation for both the trainable version and the general use of the label trick does not consistently show significant improvement, especially when considering statistical variations (e.g., standard deviation). This raises concerns about the robustness and practical impact of the proposed method.
5.  **Missing Key References and Clarity Issues:** The paper is missing important references on the subject of label propagation in graphs and the incorporation of label data. Specifically, "Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes" by Arthur D. Szlam, Mauro Maggioni, and Ronald R. Coifman (2008) is a crucial omission. Furthermore, descriptions of algorithms, such as C&S, are not sufficiently self-contained, making it difficult for readers to fully understand them without external consultation.

### Suggestions for Improvement

1.  **Clarify Motivation:** Provide a much clearer and more robust motivation for the deterministic label trick, explicitly addressing its potential advantages in terms of computational speed, accuracy, stability, or other benefits compared to stochastic alternatives.
2.  **Direct Experimental Comparisons:** Conduct comprehensive direct comparisons between the proposed deterministic label trick and state-of-the-art stochastic random label tricks across various benchmarks and metrics. These comparisons are essential to demonstrate the practical efficacy and superiority (if any) of the deterministic approach.
3.  **Improve Experimental Rigor:** Enhance the experimental validation to demonstrate significant and convincing improvements, paying close attention to statistical variations (e.g., standard deviation) to ensure the robustness of the findings.
4.  **Include Missing References:** Incorporate important missing references, particularly "Regularization on Graphs with Function-adapted Diffusion Processes" by Szlam, Maggioni, and Coifman (2008), and ensure all relevant foundational work on label propagation is cited.
5.  **Enhance Algorithm Descriptions:** Make the descriptions of algorithms (e.g., C&S) more self-contained and provide clear, precise references to improve reader understanding and accessibility.

### Overall Assessment

The paper introduces a novel deterministic approach to the label trick for GNNs, supported by an interesting theoretical upper bound. However, the core motivation for this deterministic shift is underdeveloped, and the experimental section currently lacks the direct comparisons and compelling evidence needed to substantiate the practical advantages of the proposed method. Addressing the significant weaknesses in experimental validation, clarity, and foundational referencing would substantially strengthen the paper. Therefore, I rate this paper as **5: marginally below the acceptance threshold**, recommending major revisions.

title: Imitation Learning by Reinforcement Learning

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The paper contributes a theoretical analysis of imitation learning (IL) under deterministic experts. The paper shows that IL in this setting can be reduced to RL with a stationary reward, and the stationary reward minimizes the total variation distance between the expert and the learner. Several empirical experiments were presented to validate the theoretical analysis.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The paper shows that imitation learning (IL) may be reduced to RL with stationary reward for deterministic experts. The analysis provides the theoretical grounding for several existing IL methods. The paper also contributes the connection between the stationary reward to minimizing f-divergence typically seen in adversarial IL, proving that the stationary reward minimizes total variation distance.

2. The proof is clear, well motivated and easy to follow.

3. The experiment studies an important aspect of IL, which is the relationship between the amount of expert data and the IL's performance.

Weakness:
1. Similar to previous works, the practical algorithm uses soft reward (1 - min L_2 distance) to avoid reward sparsity. While a very reasonable choice, the soft reward violates the critical assumption of deterministic expert. Could the authors explain the implications of the soft reward on the theoretical results?

2. The empirical evaluation is limited, with several related questions unaddressed. For instance, the SAC expert appears to be rather weak (in terms of its performance) across different environments. It is unclear whether similar patterns would be observed if the expert matches the level of the performance observed in previous works (e.g. https://github.com/berkeleydeeprlcourse/homework/tree/master/hw1/experts). Another question is how the expert is trained has any impact on the IL performance. For instance, SAC implementation typically directly constraint the range of the action, while a Gaussian expert only clips the action as a post-processing step. In my experiences, these details have quite significant impacts on IL's performance and I hope the authors could expand on the experiments to further validate their theoretical results.

Questions:
1. Comparison between Wang et al. 2019 vs the 1-min L_2 distance reward. Could the authors motivate why the latter reward is used and how it compares to Wang et al. 2019?

2. Could the authors further elaborate on the assumption of the deterministic expert? E.g. How much does it matter in the practical algorithm? Is it even possible to differentiate between stochastic vs deterministic one from limited training data?
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper shows that, for deterministic experts, imitation learning (IL) could be reduced to RL with a stationary reward. The paper thus provides theoretical grounding for several existing works. In addition, the paper also draws connection with f-divergence minimization commonly found in adversarial IL. On the other hand, the empirical results are consistent with those from existing works, but are limited in its scope. Despite the flaws, I think the work is still interesting to the community. It would be nice if the paper could include more experiments to further support the theoretical analysis.
reviews.correctness: 3: Some of the paper’s claims have minor issues. A few statements are not well-supported, or require small changes to be made correct.
reviews.technical_novelty_and_significance: 3: The contributions are significant and somewhat new. Aspects of the contributions exist in prior work.
reviews.empirical_novelty_and_significance: 2: The contributions are only marginally significant or novel.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
